They'd Rather Fight Than Switch
nytimes_articles.abstract:
For Bob Dole, the writing may already be on the wall. 

     In 1992, the leaders of George Bush's re-election campaign engaged in a scorched-earth effort to ridicule and abuse the independent candidacy of Ross Perot. While their efforts helped drive him from the race (albeit only temporarily), they were quite successful in driving Mr. Perot's voters into the  welcoming arms of Bill Clinton. The Bush campaign broke the cardinal rule of modern politics: You can and should attack opponents, but not their supporters.

Gaming on Wall Street
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Today, the one sure tip in the stock market is to buy any company that is about to be added to the Standard & Poor's 500. The market is full of mutual and pension funds and bank trust departments that must buy stock in every company on that 500.  Each time a company is chosen to replace one that has merged or gone bust, there is a sizeable demand for its stock, even if it has just published a lousy earnings report. 

     Increasingly, the stock market itself has become just another commodity -- as its recent volatility has shown. Instead of buying the stocks of individual companies, based on earnings and performance, increasing numbers of investors buy or sell "the market." Yesterday's rise on Wall Street, like Friday's fall, shows how much the market moves on bits and pieces of information far removed from the realities of business.

Toxins at the Pump
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Carol Browner, the head of the Environmental Protection Agency, thinks the American people shouldn't be guinea pigs. She's right. 

     Ms. Browner lashed out last week at the Ethyl Corporation for introducing a fuel additive that is toxic to the brain. The chemical -- a form of manganese known as M.M.T. (for methylcyclopentadienyl manganese tricarbonyl) -- raises the octane level of gasoline. It is especially dangerous to young children.

Japan Votes for Clinton
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When forecasting the Presidential election, the punditocracy endlessly debates Bob Dole's electability, the Buchanan insurrection and Bill Clinton's resurrection from political death. But the commentators have ignored an enormously important factor: Japan. 

     The last three Presidential races were greatly influenced by events there, and this year will be no different. Washington and Tokyo have agreed on steps that could help protect President Clinton from the greatest threats to his re-election: soaring interest rates and market instability.

nytimes_articles.abstract: Lance Armstrong, usually so upbeat and optimistic, is now sounding downcast and uncertain. With some of his most important races only weeks away, he cannot tell how strong he is.. "Top form?" he repeated. "I don't know about top form."

wiki_events.headline: The Dunblane massacre leads to the death of sixteen primary school children and one teacher in Dunblane, Scotland.

pages.title: Dunblane massacre
pages.abstract: The Dunblane massacre took place at Dunblane Primary School in Dunblane, near Stirling, Scotland, on 13 March 1996, when 43-year-old Thomas Hamilton killed 16 pupils and one teacher and injured 15 others before killing himself. It remains the deadliest mass shooting in British history.

pages.title: Dunblane
pages.abstract: Dunblane is a town in the council area of Stirling, in central Scotland; it is inside the historic boundaries of the county of Perthshire. It is a commuter town, with many residents making use of good transport links to much of the Central Belt, including Glasgow and Edinburgh.
